Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.title: { 16 } - Bestiary
Book-1---Glass-Empires/Part-2---The-Worl.content:
Design Notes
General Bestiary Template
[T/N: Laid out roughly in order. See other finished samples for finished product examples.]
An entry’s Description, Wants, Intellect & Morality are invokable Facts.





























StatsHD [[HD]+[Level]] ([HP] + [Armor] AP), BR @, Size @’, Weight @#, Max. Load @#, Run @’, Fly @’, Swim @’, Dig @‘Group@ / @ / @ [T/N: Group size, as a set of possible encounter sizes, in ascending size. i.e. Solo, group/pack/murder/sounder, warband/horde.]Desc.[T/N: VERY brief physical description. 2 goals: cover anything that isn’t obvious to a GM by name/convention & provide tactile interesting fragmentary terms to bolster descriptions. no more than 200 characters.]Wants[T/N: As terse a description of the actor’s core interests as possible. i.e. golem: “Follow commandments to the letter & no further.” wolf: “Food, shelter, territory. The pack worships Tanto.“]Intellect[T/N: Terse, colorful assessment of intelligence. Clarification & instructions on handling alien neurology or behavior only if required/non-obvious. “Spells are like animals that can interpret simple speech.” “Brilliant only at solving riddles & idiotic otherwise.“]Morality[T/N: Terse, colorful assessment of morality. “Opportunistic savagery.” “Ludicrously & comically malevolent.” “Well-intentioned foppery.“]


                  
                  Translator's notes 
                  
                

HD & HP
HD is physical & spiritual durability. Humans are 2 HD & harpies, salamanders, & Coralers are all 1 HD. Otherwise, we stick kinda close to OSR, but maybe add 1 HD to normal assumptions. All HD are d6. HD ½ = 2 HP & halved rolls. HD 0 = always 0 HP. this isn’t lethal but it’s instant death if you take a hit. HP is always (HD × 4).
Level
We assume Level is 0 (& don’t bother writing it down) unless this entry is SPECIFICALLY for a ruler of a Faction (i.e. it’s a “Duke” or “General” or “Business Magnate” entry) in which case defer to core rules’ Level table to determine average assumed size.
Armor (AP)
Armor indicates absorption via Armor Points. Strictly speaking, Armor Points is a multiple of Power (HD + Level) added as an additional HP-equivalent & is based on the quality of gear. Leather is Power × 1, Chain is × 2, Plate is × 4. We can conceivably have bigger Armor bonuses for better Armor materials … but probably not.
That said, we don’t show the Armor multiplier value since it can be worked out from just the final AP number. To get the original Armor number, you can divide AP by Power.
Entities that have high HP but low HD (i.e. they are of weak character/strength of mind) should have an Armor bonus instead of getting extra HD. In addition, beasts that are literally heavily armored (like dragons) should have AP. We’d call this natural armor but we have no reason to make a distinction. In mechanical terms, some attacks can pierce Armor & attack HP directly & Armor provided from gear doesn’t heal from rest. Those are the main practical differences.
Calculating BR
As per the rules in Battles, under Battle Rating (p. @).
Size
Size is the cylindrical area you can fit into while fighting. Any value over 5’ should be rounded to 5’ increments. Standard sizes are 0’, 1’, 2’, 5’, 10’, 15’, 20’, 30’, 40’, & 60’. Arbitrarily larger sizes are also acceptable.
Weight & Max. Load
Weight is measured in stone (#). 1# = 10lb. We accept ½#, ¼#, & N/A. Should generally be rounded to increments of 3# where possible. Weight should reflect realistic mass (i.e. “this thing is 1,000 pounds” = it should be Weight 100#). Max. Load is carry capacity & should generally be 2/3 of Weight unless your knowledge of the creature’s biology for some reason makes an exception worthwhile.
Speed scores
Run, Fly, Swim & Dig (in that order) should be in 5’ increments wherever possible. Don’t list Speeds that a creature doesn’t have. Humans don’t have a Dig score. Possessing a score indicates a natural capacity for it; Humans can swim, but they don’t have Swim 15’. Climbing (i.e. like a spider) is not a separate score; it is a Feature that lets you use Run to climb. Humans move at Run 25’. We treat 5’ as 1” on a combat surface normally.
Weapon & attack design philosophy
Classic B/X or OSR design gives various monsters a bunch of redundant/similar attacks. bite+bite+claw? pls, they all do Slash damage. A weapon is meaningfully differentiated if its damage, type, usage patterns, or special effects (i.e. range, armor pierce, burning) matter. if there’s no reason to ever choose between two different weapons (natural or otherwise), why separate them?
All weapons are phrased as Features tagged with (Weapon). Natural weapons are tagged (Natural Weapon). Weapons are implicitly melee unless a range is provided (in feet, or as “LoS” for line-of-sight effects).
Weapons usually don’t grant multiple Strikes except as particularly special abilities. Extra Strikes are a separate Feature & most natural weapons should be combined into one big weapon if they have no differentiation.



Feature 1: Description of Feature. [T/N: Features can be stolen by players who cook & eat the monster. Keep that in mind while designing them. features should be simple enough that a paragraph of text fully describes their mechanics. if that’s not enough space for whatever weird shit this thing does, add it to the description & include whatever fancy tools you need i.e. md tables there.]



                  
                  T/N: Feature design philosophy 
                  
                


Simplicity over fidelity. Avoid fiddly conditionals or mental spoon load. Strive for obvious/mnemonic conditions if you need conditionals.
Avoid referencing specific weaponry unless it’s essential to the Feature. (“Natural weapons” is better than “claws”; think like, if a PC gets this power, would it make no sense? sometimes i.e. mind flayer tentacles, it’s unavoidable and that’s okay. other times, you can correct for it.)
If it only works in rare circumstances or just grants a +whatever bonus to niche actions, consider cutting it or merging it with another Feature. Each Feature should be ideally punchy and central to the thing’s identity.



[T/N: Description goes here. Very brief text description of whatever is even worth mentioning that you haven’t already. We don’t really talk about ecology or all the gay bullshit AD&D liked to bloat its page count with. Anything you put here should be witty, terse, and/or very highly relevant to what a panicked GM flipping to this page might want to know about the creature that isn’t already covered. No more than 1-2 sentences unless you really have to include a bunch of rules text that can’t be a Feature (i.e. because players should NEVER be able to get it) or if you want to do something fun like mention that you can re-use an animated snowman’s hat to animate other snow things or whatever the entry demands.]
Treasure: [Only if any consistent treasures to note; otherwise, N/A. Entries that demand interesting/legendary treasures may have a reference to a table i.e. “See table.” plus adjacent table(s) of treasures.]


                  
                  T/N: Optional: GM Prep Tables 
                  
                

Determine the most difficult part of using the creature. Basically, ask yourself “what’s the hardest thing for a GM to make up on the spot that will almost always be needed with this creature?” pick the top 0-3. Sphinxes need riddles & GMs never have a book of riddles on-hand. We want to maximize utility. a prep table should NEVER consist of:

Mundanities (the dwarf has a family)
easy stereotypes (the dwarf is a miner with a beard & is named greyhammer & lives in a hill)
random bullshit (the dwarf is a gay cake-maker)

it should be structured, difficult-to-ad-hoc content with extremely high conceptual density. whatever’s critical to its identity & hard to come up with. “Do the hard work for the GM.”
some ideas: “What is this thing doing?” “What is its nature?” “Cause/casus belli for [stupid shit they do but that’s difficult to wrap your head around as a human who doesn’t think like they do]”. victims/slaves/captives, symbiotes/parasites & other common/universal relationships, “why the fuck would we ever bother this thing/who’s prompting us into it & why”, “common/interesting variants or very similar creatures you can make by changing this thing’s name & giving it some minor tweaks that you can contain in 160-200 characters”, “organizational structure”, “premade encounters”, “hazards that are required to go along with this entity to make it work conceptually”, “weird weapons & armor”, “WHY the FUCK does this thing EXIST, who did this and why???”, “set of specific highly interesting & thematically appropriate loot”, “legendary/mythical powers associated with this creature or with special/unique versions of it”.
write tables as proper Markdown tables. don’t bother giving them headings since you can just put the heading into the table itself like the examples below. you can include multiple columns if they all go together i.e. “Gem color” “Value” “Power” or whatever. we only use d6 dice, so 1d6 for most tables, or 2d6 if you need lots of results or weighted results. you can also use d66 numbered double per entry i.e. 11–12, 13–14 […] 65–66 to get an 18-entry table w/ a flat curve … but you’d better have a really exciting reason to make an 18-entry table.




                  
                  Optional: When eaten 
                  
                

Flavor: [Terse, colorful description of flavor or experience of consumption. “Disgustingly fatty pork.” “Like eating coal, because you are, and it’s just as unhealthy.“]
Notes: [Description of effects, either as a flat-out “this happens” or “Roll on table below.” followed by a table (1d6 or 2d6) of 2-4 side effects. Side effects should be equally balanced by permanence & positive/negative effects. They can just outright kill the players via poison or horrible nightmare magical fate if that’s appropriate for the creature in question. You can include qualifiers.]




                  
                  T/N: Regarding When Eaten entries 
                  
                

Players can eat monsters to gain better stats (if it had better stats than them) & their Features or Facts (as mutations, kinda). If a creature has other unusual effects or characteristics related to its consumption (i.e. some beasts are HORRIBLY CURSED or KNOWN TO CURE DISEASE or THE FEAST OF KINGS), that’s what this section is for. It should NOT be included for anything of blisteringly mundane quality. Basically, if you’re describing a normal meal, this section should not be here. A goat entry doesn’t deserve a When Eaten section (unless it’s a Scapegoat & eating it transfers all the awful sins the goat bears onto the consumer or some shit).
write tables as proper Markdown tables. don’t nest rolls inside them. Don’t use Saves, just do straight 1d6 or 2d6 tables with entries weighted appropriately.




                  
                  T/N: Other general design principles 
                  
                


Try not to use funny names where English words or calques would be better. Names should be mnemonic where possible; trivial to remember.
Natural weapons are not Features for the sake of BR. No weapon is.
We do some lumping. Basically, ask yourself if a creature could be achieved with a very minor edit to the stats of this creature. A name edit, a tweak to a stat or two, maybe one extra ability. The giant badger is a slight edit of the wolf. The dolphin is a slight edit of the seal.

Tables of variant creatures should never include the base creature. If a GM wanted to use the creature as-is, he’d just do that.


Generally speaking, pack hunters don’t need abilities that specifically represent their skill for hunting in packs; this is implicit to the way the Battles system works. A pack has a huge advantage in claiming tactical bonuses (terrain, perception, flanking, maneuverability, morale) which can potentially add up to a +4 collectively (on a 2d6 Save!). This could allow a relatively small pack to fight on even ground against a creature with up to 4× more BR than the pack does. Ergo, don’t give a “pack hunter” bonus unless it’s really, sincerely justified.
AP must always be granted by an Armor score. If you want to give a creature an ablative pool of HP somehow (i.e. a robot having parts with separate HP), you should give it fixed HP and/or HD.
Immunities, Resistances, and Weaknesses are conveyed via Features because we want them to be copiable by alchemy. (Every Feature is potentially an ability that a player could obtain.)
Unusual/magical equipment is listed as a Feature, tagged with (Equipment), i.e. “Magic Shield (Equipment)“.

Unusual/magical weapons are similarly tagged as {Weapon}.
Unusual natural weapons are tagged as (Natural Weapon).
Class Features (i.e. derived from profession or knowledge) are labeled (Class) & are otherwise presumed to be Racial (or natural) Features.





Description, Wants, Intellect, & Morality can all be invoked as Facts where necessary.

@ Our Fellow Glassian
Commoner





























StatsHD 2 (8), BR 2, Size 5’, Weight 15#, Max. Load 10#, Run 25’GroupSolo / 1d6 household / 2d6 work gang / 3d6 × 10 angry mobDesc.Calloused hands, sunburnt face. Carries tools or a basket. Moves with purpose.WantsStability, prosperity, & to be left the hell alone. Failing that, revenge.IntellectShrewd about anything they can see & touch. Credulous about anything they can’t.MoralityGenerous in times of plenty, ruthless in scarcity. Always hedging.

Improvised Weapon (Weapon): 4 Physical. Hatchet, mattock, hammer, kitchen knife, or whatever’s at hand.

Grand & free Glass — the Commoner will sing to its history, wherever they hold a voice to sing with, for they believe themselves its blood & soul. And a betrayed blood & soul!
Commoners will rarely attack directly, but when they do, they attack with ruthless mania. They feel emotions stronger than you do. The most dangerous thing is a tight-knit village that’s got nothing left to lose.
Treasure: 1d6−2 in pocket money. A household has 3d6 × 10 in savings, tools, home & trade goods investments, for a plundering bandit.


                  
                  Playing a Glassian Commoner 
                  
                

These are not medieval serfs. Kain’s long reign suppressed the worst excesses of intermediate lordship. Glassian commoners are commercially literate, own property, haggle confidently, & hold strong opinions about the quality of their local products versus the next village’s. They remember prosperity & are furious about losing it.
Spirits are landlords. Commoners negotiate with river spirits the way you negotiate with your landlords. They set out offering plates at tree roots, consult the shrine-keeper before building an extension, & teach their children which mushrooms have spirits in them.
Glassians know their trade, their village, their local spirits & rumors from the last trader or harpy courier who came through. They don’t know politics or how magic works, but they’re not stupid. Everything they know works because it worked for the last thousand years.
Glassians are not neutral. Under competing warlords, commoners hedge between factions the way a trader diversifies debts. They hide grain, maintain polite relations with whoever’s currently armed, & gossip as intelligence-gathering. They will sell information for safety. Their children are simultaneously their retirement, labor force, & army.










































































































































































































































































d66MaleFemaleOccupationPossession11FenLarkRice farmerJade amulet from before the war12ColeSedgeOrchardistKnife, clearly military salvage13AshPearlTea growerGrandmother’s spirit-ward14TamRueSilkworm keeperToken of debt from a failed credit union15RillEmberCharcoal burnerLetter they can’t read, wax-sealed16GarthVineRiver fishermanSeeds wrapped in oilcloth, hoarded21HoltCoralPotterBrass bell for spirit offerings22KeelBrineWeaverHalf-empty medicine pouch23MossFernDyerCeramic flask of plum wine24BreckHazelCarpenterIron nails & hammer25SladeIvyCooperRolled warrant with a Lord’s seal26MarshAsterTannerSack of rice that isn’t theirs31BrenDuskBrewerHarpy feather & knot in hat32TarnShaleFerrymanWorn prayer beads for a dead god33CulmReedCarter & porterPouch of market-day coins ($1d6)34DrayPlumMarket stall keeperBeautiful hat. Very proud of it.35FlintCloveInnkeeperPortable clay spirit-house36GaleSorrelTeahouse operatorSomeone else’s family seal; why?41HartTealShrine keeperRope, bundled42JemAlderSpirit-ward braiderOffering bowl & morning rice43KnoxCressMidwife & herbalistHerbs, bundled44LanDaleBee-keeperDeed to cottage & farm45NollElmPig-keeperLive belligerent chicken46PikeFlaxSalt-makerStolen military rations51RoanGlenRope-makerOld flute52TorIrisThatcherCeramic honey jug53VossMyrtleKiln-workerHand-drawn local map54WardSageHarpy-mail clerkIncense sticks for offerings55CadeWrenIron wholesalerSingle ancient tool, treated like gold56LorneOpalBath-house keeperBasket of fresh vegetables61SettBirchFestival organizerOut-of-season festival decorations62ThaneLeaMilitia drill leaderShield, salvaged & dented.63OakKitWall-menderBag of lime morite & trowel64MaceRueScavenger/rag-pickerHandful of bent nails & scrap metal65WrenNettieGrave-diggerSmall knife & whetstone66YewJuneNight-soil collectorNothing but worn, ragged clothes













































2d6Surname convention2−3Debt. Embarrassing outstanding obligation. “Fen Owes-the-Shrine.”4Ancestor. Named for a supposedly embodied ancestor. Roll again for regular name. “Aster Pearl.”5Deed. Something they or an ancestor did. “Ward Bridgebuilder,” “Flint Pigkiller.” Almost never chosen; imposed by the community.6None. Just a given name.7Family trade. “Breck Cooper” or “Dusk Dyer.” Family trade changes, but names don’t, which causes confusion.8Village. “Keel of Oxbow” or “Sedge Millford.” Used mainly by people who’ve moved. Marks you as an outsider wherever you go except home.9Beast. Named for a creature. “Hart Boar.” “Lark Crane.” May be subtly mutated to resemble it from extensive dynastic ritual consumption.10Patronymic. “Gale Fernson” or “Cress Tamson.” Roll again for father’s name & append “-son”.11−12Landmark. “Tor Cliff” or “Glen Ford.” Implies the family is that place; they maintain it, they’re responsible for it, & they’d die for it.

















































































d66Commoner problems11–12Son (or daughter) conscripted by force.13–14Daughter ran off with a merc company. She chose to go. Parents furious, ashamed.15–16Creditor calling in debts the family can’t pay. Debt slavery may be forced.21–22Kid found a staircase in a dry well. Has started sleepwalking toward it.23–24Bandits (p. @) on the road demanding tolls. Too organized to be desperate.25–26Neighbor accused of feeding an evil spirit. Village split on topic of exile.31–32Local magistrate (p. @) vanished a week ago during water rights crisis.33–34Harpy courier (p. @) wrongly delivered letter with dangerous contents.35–36Family member ate something bad & is mutating. Won’t discuss what.41–42Way too many daughters & nobody to marry them to.43–44Soldiers (p. @) quartered in the village refuse to leave. Eating everything.45–46Cow dying of a disease. Herbalist & Jester disagree how; both want payment.51–52Someone’s robbing grave offerings. The dead are restless. Help!53–54The wind spirit won’t move the windmill anymore. Says the contract is void!55–56Fire destroyed the granary. There’s no reserves.61–62Strangers are lurking & asking odd pointed questions. We’re being cased!63–64Two villages in a feud over a shared resource (ford, mill, grazing land). This commoner is trying to broker peace before it turns bloody.65–66Something in the forest ate our horses. The tracks are strange.

















































































d66Mood & demeanor11–12Grim endurance. Treats the war like weather. Terrible, inevitable, endured.13–14Manic commerce. Selling everything not nailed down while coins still matter.·15–16Doubled faith. Tripled offerings to the spirits. Judges anyone impious.21–22Simmering fury. At the current lord, at the war, at armed strangers, at everything.23–24Performing fine. Insists everything is normal. Smile doesn’t reach the eyes.25–26Drunk all day. Still working, but starting to fray around the edges.31–32Deep suspicion. Asks questions. Watching your hands. Counting your weapons.33–34Desperate hospitality. Overfeeding you. Insisting you stay. Lonely & terrified.35–36Bitter clarity. Too smart for hope. Brilliant & ambitious if you can motivate them.41–42Gossip engine. Everything you say is transmitted to everyone within a day’s walk.43–44Scheming. Working an angle. Sizing you up for usefulness & mark-status.45–46Emptied out. Too exhausted to be scared. No energy left for lies.51–52Nostalgic. Won’t stop talking about how much better things were under Kain.53–54Protective rage. Someone loved is in danger. Will do anything to keep them safe.55–56Unnerving cheer. Laughing at everything. Famine is more tolerable with a jest.61–62Hedging. Polite relations with two or more factions. Entire village follows lead.63–64Displaced. Not from here. Visibly doesn’t fit. Trying to be useful enough to stay.65–66Frozen. Waiting. For news, for the war to end. Can’t make decisions. Needs a push.

















































































d66Village landmark & pride11–12Negotiations-friendly teahouse.13–14Stone bridge, collectively built.15–16Hot spring & bathhouse.21–22Famous kiln employs half the village.23–24Market square under massive oak.25–26Watermill; processes all grains..31–32Shrine & annual festival to local hero.33–34Only Jester for miles.35–36Famous brewery.41–42Eccentric Lord’s huge manor.43–44Beautiful terraced hill gardens.45–46Old stone wall from Kain’s era.51–52Bee-keeping collective.53–54Prosperous silk workshop.55–56Locally-known cave system.61–62Enormous communal granary.63–64Natural harbor, fishing fleet.65–66Standing stone circle.
@ Burgher





























StatsHD 2 (8), BR 4, Size 10’, Weight 90#, Max. Load 60#, Run 50’GroupSolo / 2d6 herd / 3d6 × 10 cavalryDesc.WantsIntellectMorality
Entrepreneurs & artisans; the middle & upper economic/mercantile class. Serves as the primary source of new Magistrates.
Horse





























StatsHD 3 (12), BR 4, Size 10’, Weight 90#, Max. Load 60#, Run 50’GroupSolo / 2d6 herd / 3d6 × 10 cavalryDesc.Long legs, alert ears. Glossy coat, nervous eyes. Snorts & whinnies when alarmed.WantsGrass, water, the herd. A rider who knows what they’re doing.IntellectRead emotion & intent. Can be trained extensively but panic easily.MoralityFlight before fight. Loyal to those who treat them well.

Hooves (Natural Weapon): 5 Physical.

StatsHD 2/4/8, BR 3/12/48, Size 5’/10’/20’, Weight N/A, Max. Load N/A, Run 5’GroupSwarm (HD 2, Size 5’) / Horde (HD 4, Size 10’) / Plague (HD 8, Size 20’)Desc.Seething carpet of countless tiny bodies. Chittering, rustling, overwhelming.WantsFood. Kill enemies. Bites & Stings (Natural Weapon): Physical/Poison. Damage equal to your HD.
Swarm: You gain all of the following effects:

You’re composed of a large swarm of tiny entities.
You are functionally immune to any special effect that only affects a single target.
Damage that only affects a single target is reduced to 1.
You can move through any gap a single member could fit through.
You cannot be Grappled, shoved, knocked Prone, or prevent characters from moving into or through the space you occupy.
Damage from area effects is multiplied by your current HD before being applied.


Swarming Strikes: You may make 1 Strike per Attack against every creature Engaged with you or within your occupied area simultaneously.

When reduced to 0 HP, the horde “dies” & disperses—survivors scatter into cracks and shadows. A horde at half HP or less reduces its Size by one category (20’→10’→5’) as its numbers thin.
Treasure: N/A (though their nest may hold valuables from victims)

Spider Lily





























StatsHD 1 (4), BR 2, Size 2’, Weight 1/4#, Run 25’Group2d6Desc.Pale red egg-body on ten spindly red legs. Thick long brown proboscis underneath supports the body while feeding. Padded feet, silent skitter. Faint sweet stink.WantsCorpses. If there are none, it will make some.IntellectNotices only warmth, moisture, & decay.MoralityHunger, sans haste.

Proboscis (Natural Weapon): 2 Poison.
Miasma: When you remain stationary for a minute or more, you emit a poisonous haze in a 10’ radius. Creatures in the area take 1 Poison Damage per minute, starting as soon as they enter, stacking with other nearby lilies. Sleeping or unconscious creatures don’t notice the damage until they wake (or don’t).
Root: You can root in any corpse or helpless creature. While rooted, heal 1 HP per hour. Uprooting takes 1 Round.

The Jesters say the spider lilies are born from where the world of the dead draws close. They are correct in the sense that spider lilies create the world of the dead.
Spider lily clusters migrate toward warmth & the smell of the living. Villages hire exterminators. Armies post sentries over the dead. In this age of war, nobody sleeps near an unburied corpse.

































1d6What did they grow from?1Shallow roadside grave. Fresh. Personal effects still on the body. Someone’s searching for the corpse.2Dead livestock in a pen. Owner can’t afford the loss of the meat & begs you not to burn them.3Under a building’s floorboards. Inhabitants are pale, coughing, don’t understand why. Someone died in the crawlspace weeks ago.4Battlefield dead in a depression. Hundreds of lilies across the field. Gear is salvageable if you can clear them.5A living person, asleep in their bed. Proboscises embedded in their skin. Still breathing. Removing the lilies will kill them faster than leaving them.6A cracked tomb or shrine. Whatever was sealed inside is no longer whole. Local Jester wants it resealed but won’t go near it.

































1d6Why it’s even worse1Someone important lives in the infested building and refuses to leave.2The locals claim the lilies are sacred; the spirit world is speaking through them. Destroying them would be sacrilege.3The lilies have rooted into a well or stream & are poisoning everything downstream.4Someone is cultivating them deliberately as a weapon of war.5The dead person had something valuable on them. It’s in the middle of the cluster.6What you see is the edge. The infestation stretches underground & through connected structures.


                  
                  When eaten 
                  
                

Notes: Crunchy, silken limbs like bitter dandelions; egg-like body is thick like offal.
Effects: If the egg is eaten, Save or die in 1d6 hours from horrible, painful, bloody vomiting. Legs are edible.

Bound: Once per Turn as a Free Action, leap up to 15’ in any direction.

Imported to Glass from Inner Sea archipelagos as novelty hunting game by bored Lords. Their populations exploded in coastal marshlands after Coraler invasion disrupted control efforts. Now they’re a fixture of the wetlands—and occasionally, a shrine-guardian or omen-bearer.
Jesters claim the toads bear messages from water-spirits. Whether this is true or convenient superstition remains unclear, but disturbing a toad’s pond is considered inviting misfortune.

The victor will be decided by water.5Soft, almost musical croaking: A treasure or secret lies within the pond’s vicinity. The toad guards it.6Complete silence when it should croak: The toad has been claimed by something worse. Leave immediately and do not look back.

StatsHD 2 (8), BR 4, Size 5’, Weight 9#, Max. Load 6#, Run 5’Group1d6 sprout / 3d6 bloomDesc.Man-sized white bell-capped mushroom. thin like an umbrella on gliding millipede mycelium feet. Silent Samba: You can move absolutely silently (excepting gear).
Sporeburst: Poisonous spores grow under the bellcap attached to your head. When within 5’ of a living animal, the bellcap automatically snaps up like a broken umbrella & releases a poisonous black cloud. The cloud starts as a 10’ radius cloud centered on you & expands in radius by 5’ per Round, up to 30’. It lingers for an hour.

Any breathing creature within the cloud takes 4 Poison Damage at the start of each of their Turns. If they die from this poison, their corpse sprouts 1d6 Creeping Caps in 1d6 days.
After this Feature is triggered, unless you have a fresh corpse to feed from, you die in 1 day.


Creeping Cap Resistances: Immune to Poison; resistant to Cold; weak to Hot.

There is little worse than waking up to find several “creepers” sitting outside your home’s every exit, waiting for your door to open — a single mushroom can sit there for weeks before dropping dead. They prefer cornering prey in burrows, caves, or other tight spaces where they can’t escape.
Treasure: Creeping Cap stalks are a delicacy worth $500 each.

Formidian Strength: You are unaffected by encumbrance & may carry up to your full Max. Load without suffering any ill effects.
Alarm Pheromones: When you’re attacked for the first time, there’s a 3-in-6 chance that 3d6 ants who share a colony with you within a 100’ radius will converge on your location to attack the source of the attack.
Colony Pheromones: You produce pheromones that, when rubbed on people or objects, marks them as part of the colony (for people) or to be carried back to the colony (for objects) by workers or soldiers.

Giant ant colonies are the boon & bane alike of the kingdoms where they settle. If they had any diplomatic sense to commit to negotiations, they might be practical partners — but their queens are mad & their princes & princesses dangerous, fickle creatures.
The average ant has a Battle Rating of 7.26. A regular colony of 100 ants has a BR of 726.
Treasure: 3d6 × $100 in loose coin & valuables stored within the colony’s garbage chamber.

Illusion: You are not incorporeal. You aren’t even undead. You don’t exist at all. You are a projection of light & sound from a corpse-body serving as a Totem, housing a trapped & tortured spirit. If your Totem is destroyed, you revert to being a normal loose spirit.

Ghosts aren’t monsters. They’re psychic splatters of horrible violent deaths … or deliberate traps made by unscrupulous magicians. Their manifestation is a shattered vision of whatever transpired.
A ghost is composed of 3 components: The Totem, generally within 30’; the premonition, an emotional or physical flash of warning; and the effect.
Treasure: Sometimes, a dead body to be looted (p. @).

Nine Inch Nails (Natural Weapon): 4 Physical.
Extra Strike: You get an extra Strike per Attack made with any weapon.
Reanimation: Once per hour, as a Full-Turn Action, animate a reasonably intact corpse as an Undead (p. @). If you have the control capability for it, it’s under your control.
Undead Commander: You may hold control of up to 10 × [HD] × [HD] in BR of mindless undead at one time.
Undead: You are a moving corpse. You gain all of the following modifiers:

You can see spirited creatures as a dim outline. Most animals fear & hate you.
You need no food, sleep, or air. Consequentially, while unencumbered, you can travel 24 hours per day — or at 3 times any of your speed scores in miles per day.
You are immune to Poison Damage & resistant to Mind Damage.
You are immune to morale, fear, & panic.

the remnants of the old civilization from before the Blooming. most of them still recall it. put you to sleep. seize control, possess you from up to a mile away. continually farm & consume cave lotus leaves (subterranean, extremely hard/slow to grow) to stay asleep. continually replenishes magical power while asleep. they built the templevaults. palaces of the mind.
cyclolucidites + mind flayers. ancient evil masterminds. ruled the world. slowly waking up.
@ Dwarf
the dwarves are gone. there is no such thing as a dwarf. you cannot see the dwarf. the dwarves do not exist. they are all gone. they are all dead. they are crawling in your blood. there are no dwarves here. you will leave because there is nobody watching you. you will not be followed and watched because they are all dead. you could bring them back. your hand moves by your own accord.
you should bring them back.

Something that is & always was unreal can still find a way to cast a shadow.

Imperial Court
True Immortal





























StatsHD 2d6 (8–24), BR ∞, Size 5’, Weight 60#, Max. Load 40#, Run 25’, Fly 250’GroupSolo / Cult patron (Level 2d6, Godhead enables summoning)Desc.A radiant, hyper-real human, possibly disguised as a lesser being.WantsInscrutably mad whims fulfilled. An end to the ennui.IntellectBrilliant but ordinary human, augmented by magic or tools.MoralityTwisted into knots by uncountable & unaccountable aeons.

Hourai Immortal: You are a true immortal & are subject to the following conditions:

Your body replaces limbs, implants, Mutations, Wounds, new HD or Racial Features, & other abnormalities to perfectly restore itself to ordinary functionality within 24 hours of any damage or change being inflicted.
If killed, in 1d6 Rounds, you immolate into ash & reconstitute instantly in a burst of light & cold flame at a place of your choosing within 100’, displacing or destroying any material present if no empty space is available.
You are always conscious, even in death, & as a Free Action may choose to die & begin resurrecting.
Your soul is not in your body; it is beyond the cycle of life & death. No effect targeting a soul works on you.
You are still subject to all natural needs & vulnerabilities & have no special resistances to Damage, but you regenerate fast enough that only an absence of air will kill you.
This Feature can never be copied, borrowed, stolen, given, removed, inherited, overruled, etc. by any means whatsoever.



Even an undying force, so long as it be carved in the shape of Man, can be swayed & broken. A god with boundaries is one that can be defeated. A human plus eternity is a predictable cycle.
An immortal knows every trade, has seen all of history, knows a man’s inner character better than they do. You would have to be desperate or stupid to get in the way of an immortal … & there are plenty of desperate & stupid people in the world.
To generate an immortal, roll on the tables until you have a coherent picture. Pilfer liberally from other bestiary entries. Assume enormous competence, but play fairly. Immortals are still human & they aren’t always smart — just very well-learned, with vast resources.
Treasure: A slain immortal may carry a treasure & have 1d4−1 hidden elsewhere. It may not willingly relinquish them.

























































d66Current project11–13Trying to die for good. Heard there was a method. Testing it out. Collateral damage is mounting? Who cares?14–16Waiting for an event. Political confluence, meeting, stars’ alignment. It was calculated centuries ago. Don’t distract me.21–23Brokering a treaty. Acting as a neutral party. Both sides hate the other & will pay happily for anyone who can corrupt the immortal or destroy the other side’s bargaining position.24–26Rebuilding. There used to be a monument here. A palace, a canal, a machine. All this will go, one way or another.31–33Hiding. May be on the run from the Lunar Princess (p. @). Scared badly enough that they won’t tell you what. You might die for discovering them.34–36Governing. Or rather, I’m just that good. They worship me. They do everything I say. What, you want something? Are you gonna get in the way?41–43Collecting. Every left shoe in the kingdom. All of the nobles’ eyeballs. Every book containing an old king’s name.44–46Teaching a single mortal. He’s becoming dangerously powerful & terrifically unaccountable. Good!51–53Feuding with another neighboring immortal. They enjoy the game. Two kingdoms exist entirely to sustain the conflict. Every proximal relationship is another front.54–56Familicide. Systemically hunting down an entire bloodline across a continent.61–63Sleeping. Everyone is terrified of the awakening. Nobody remembers what this one was like.64–66Prolonging a war. Might be for entertainment; may be for a scheme. Might be testing the limits of a participant, or pressuring them.

























































d66Immortal abilities11–13True Teleportation: As a Major Action, instantly move to anywhere you can see or have been before.14–16Blood Memory: As a Major Action, shapeshift into any humanoid form you have previously touched. The disguise is physically perfect. Features are not mimicked.21–23Perfect Memory: You recall every face, word, & event you have ever witnessed. You can reproduce any document, map, or conversation verbatim. You cannot forget, even if you wish to.24–26Undeniable: You may choose to be impossible to ignore. At any time, no creature within line-of-sight or earshot can look away, leave, or take any action not directed at you.31–33Spirit Sight: You can see invisible entities, the structure of spirits, enchantments, illusions, & everything else as they truly are; nothing is hidden from your direct sight.34–36Compulsion of Truth: Anyone speaking directly to you cannot knowingly lie, even by omission. They may refuse to speak. This is not magic; it is the sheer weight of your attention.41–43Iron Fortitude: You do not tire, hunger, thirst, or need sleep, though you may choose to. You can march, work, or fight indefinitely without penalty.44–46Thousand Names: You possess valid, established identities in dozens of cultures, courts, & institutions across the Disc. You can arrive almost anywhere and be recognized as someone important, expected & trusted.51–53Prophecy: Once per month, you may ask the GM a single yes/no question about the future. The GM answers truthfully based on his understanding of current trajectories. The future is not fixed.54–56Master of All: You have mastered every mundane trade, art, and science known to civilization. Given materials & time, you can build or create anything a perfectionist master might.61–63Weather Controller: Command the weather within three miles’ radius to anything desired with minutes of notice. Summon hurricanes, snowstorms, rainstorms, or tornadoes.64–66Pronounce Death: Once per day, speak aloud a character’s true name & the deadly fate they’ll meet. If it’s possible that they meet it in the next 6 minutes & it affects nobody else, they meet it. No Save. Works on everything, including elementals.






































































d66DispositionSwirling legends11–13FuriousOnce sank a fleet by standing on the shore & screaming.14–16AmiableTaught the first salamanders how to smelt bronze.21–23IrritableCursed a bloodline for a thousand years over an insult at dinner.24–26InquisitiveHas asked every mortal they’ve met the same question. No one has answered correctly yet. Nobody remembers the question.31–33DismissiveUsed to rule a kingdom. Left because it bored them. The kingdom still exists & claims them as patron.34–36PompousWrote the foundational legal code of a civilization that collapsed centuries ago. Still quotes it.41–43MelacholicWas last seen weeping over a grave so old the name has weathered away.44–46PlayfulRegularly disguises themselves as a beggar & tests the hospitality of settlements. Rewards & punishes accordingly.51–53ParanoidHas not been seen in decades. The last person who found them was returned alive but unable to speak.54–56GenerousGives gifts freely … but every gift creates an obligation. The immortal remembers every debt.61–63ColdSpeaks of mortal lives the way a farmer speaks of seasons.64–66ManicAlternates between frenzied activity & catatonic withdrawal on a cycle no one has been able to predict.

























































d66Peculiarities & proclivations11–13Genuinely all-loving. Would probably help if not already busy & if aid wouldn’t hurt anyone. Never helps for free.14–16Distant ancestor of a PC. Wants them to be more alike.21–23Recently became immortal. Doesn’t remember how. Wants — needs — to find out.24–26Trapped in an intangible state. Can only interact with world via Godhead (p. @). Would like to restore physical body.31–33Collects mortal apprentices. Teaches them everything, then abandons them at the peak of their ability. Former students are scattered across the world, many in positions of power, all with complicated feelings about their teacher.34–36Has fallen in love with a mortal. Knows they will die. This has happened before. It always ends the same way. The immortal does it anyway.41–43Maintains a personal archive of every text they’ve ever read. Will do almost anything to acquire one they lack. Will kill to prevent one being destroyed.44–46Has sworn a binding oath to another immortal & regrets it. The terms constrain their actions in ways they refuse to explain.51–53Is playing both sides of the current conflict. Has real affection for people on each side. Cannot bring themselves to choose.54–56Lives as a mortal. New name, new trade, new town every few decades. Currently deep in a life they care about, with a spouse, children, neighbors who will grow old & die. Reacts to exposure/recognition the way a cornered animal reacts to a torch.61–63Convinced it’s being tested by an older, more powerful immortal. Can’t prove it. Paranoia manifests as irrational behavior.64–66Ridiculously formal. Demands letters & formal proclamations. Keeps a massive calendar of debts, which substitutes for a moral compass.

























































d66Immortal treasures11–13Apple of Mania. Everyone who lays eyes upon it desires it. The desire is irrational, immediate, equal in strength to the character’s greatest desire. & does not diminish with distance or time. The immortal is immune only by familiarity.14–16The Last Letter. A sealed document from someone who died. Never opened, never will be. Not dangerous for what it says; dangerous for what the immortal will do if it is threatened or destroyed.21–23Unbreakable Chain. A completely indestructible 244’ chain of thin adamantine. It weighs 1#.24–26The Antidote. A cure for the current worst thing. The immortal won’t use it because the last time, the consequences were worse than the disease.31–33The Elixir. The singular source of Hourai Immortality. Intended to be delivered to a specific person as a gift. One use; the entire container must be consumed.34–36Hunting Horn. If blown loudly, this immortal will immediately appear. It’s a voluntary thing. It will respect the horn.41–43Necklace of Ears. Literally made from severed ears. The wearer can understand every language, including animal emotions, but not speak them.44–46Spare Fate. A single slim red thread. Can be sacrificed to miraculously invert a fate that has just been sealed — a king’s declaration of war, a love lost, your own death.51–53Tianshima. A brass & redwood treasure junk, sans sails. Given a crew of 20, it can sail through the sky. Holds 6,000# in cargo. One-of-a-kind anomaly. Currently in use by the immortal.54–56Small metal tablet. Hand-sized, scuffed. Glassy panel on one surface; if touched, a trapped fairy mathematician appears to provide guidance.61–63Martyr’s Bracelet. Jagged thorns dig into skin. If an ally within line-of-sight or 1,000’ would suffer a negative effect, you may transfer its full effect onto yourself.64–66Respect. This immortal is borderline-undefeated. If you can kill it, it will perform a single favor for you to the spirit of your word & the limits of its abilities.


                  
                  When Eaten 
                  
                

Flavor: Bitter pork. Degenerates gradually into ash over 24 hours.
Notes: The Hourai Immortal Feature cannot be obtained, but other Features rolled on the Abilities table can be gained via the normal consumption rules. Even still, the immortal will reconstitute, remember who ate them, & have opinions about it.


Lunarians
The Lunarians do not have an “economy” & are not best understood as a Faction, but rather instead as a den of powerful spirits (the Royal Family) overseeing a collection of pets (the moon rabbits) in a kind of post-scarcity bubble society (the Lunar Kingdom) on a bizarre & hostile world (the Moon) full of their scattered, experimental, discarded, or disabled weaponry & utilities (the Lunarian flora & fauna, plus their artifacts).
The moon rabbits rarely deal with the Lunar ecosystem directly. They have spirits who keep the worst of the Moon’s hazards away from their villages.
Moon Rabbit Operator





























StatsHD 1 (4), BR 12, Size 5’, Weight 12#, Max. Load 8#, Run 35’Group1d6+1 away team / 3d6 squadDesc.Neotenous human with rabbit ears, tail & legs. Always dressed in camouflage or (bad) disguise suitable for the environment.WantsVegetables. Not to be discovered.IntellectFollows orders with such literal precision it loops back to stupidity.MoralityThe mission is morally correct because it is the mission.

Fists (Natural Weapon): 2 Physical.
Jade Pestle (Weapon): 1/4#, 1H, 4 Physical.
Ray Gun (Weapon): 1#; 2H; 15 Fire/Ice.
Ray Shield (Equipment): 1/4#. Immune to all Damage until struck by an effect that deals 6 or more Damage, at which point the shield gem instantly shatters, destroying the item (& fully absorbing that particular effect’s Damage).
Moon Telepathy: You are Immune to Mind Damage. You can broadcast & receive speech as if talking to everyone with Moon Telepathy up to at most a 1-mile radius. You can dim the range or mute your ability to hear other broadcasts.
Rabbit Instincts: You must Save to not Panic from unexpected loud noises, dogs, or eagles (outside ongoing combat). You must Save to not investigate interesting smells.
Bound: Once per Turn as a Free Action, leap up to 15’ in any direction.
Rabbit Guard’s Feet: You never make sounds while moving. You can jump hard enough to move between the Moon & Aia (takes 1 day) provided that a landing point on the Moon (or Aia) is currently visible. You cannot reach anywhere else with this ability.

The Lunar Kingdom hates everyone on Aia. If moon rabbits show up at your doorstep, you are being incompetently assassinated.
Moon rabbits are excellent at holding defensive positions because they naturally freeze when threatened.
Treasure: Jade name tag ($30), 2d6 cakes.

































1d6Current Mission1Retrieve a lunar artifact. They have a sketch but cannot identify it. Will attempt to take anything vaguely similar back to the Moon, because they are all tired. The artifact has a 3-in-6 chance of even existing nearby & is extremely dangerous & powerful if it does.2Observe and report. They are observing. They have been observing for three weeks. Nobody told them what to report or how & everyone’s afraid to ask.3Secure the perimeter. They have formed a perfect circle around something strategically valuable & will not let anyone cross it, including each other. They are now split across both sides of their own perimeter.4Punish the random Aian who offended the Princess. They have a name but no description. They will ask every person they meet “Are you [name]?“5Training exercise. You are part of the training simulation & are the targets.6Lost. Their commander received contradictory orders and they are now marching in a perfect holding pattern until resolution.
@ Moon Rabbit





























StatsHD 1 (4), BR 0.5, Size 5’, Weight 12#, Max. Load 8#, Run 35’Group1d6+1 fluffle / 3d6×10 warrenDesc.Neotenous human with rabbit ears, tail & legs, friendly smile. Light, casual, brightly-patterned clothes.WantsVegetables. To be sociable. Gossip & rumors. To cause trouble & confusion for fun.IntellectVery literal. Diligent & systemic. Prone to logician’s fallacies.MoralityLaid-back & easy-going. Genuinely well-intentioned.

Fists (Natural Weapon): 2 Physical.
Moon Telepathy: You are Immune to Mind Damage. You can broadcast & receive speech as if talking to everyone with Moon Telepathy up to at most a 1-mile radius. You can dim the range or mute your ability to hear other broadcasts.
Bound: Once per Turn as a Free Action, leap up to 15’ in any direction.
Rabbit Instincts: You must Save to not Panic from unexpected loud noises, dogs, or eagles (outside ongoing combat). You must Save to not investigate interesting smells.

Unchanging aeons of relative ease in Lunar cities has divorced them from the realities of life. They know almost nothing about Aia & are astonished (& easily entranced) by stories of war, conquest, famine, charisma, tournaments, politics, monster-slaying, etc. They will follow interesting people around asking lots of questions & then broadcast the answers to everyone.
Moon rabbits are immortal. On the Moon, they have a half-life of around 500 years.
Treasure: Jade coins (3d6 × $10).

































1d6What they want from you1Stories about Aia. Will trade local information for entertainment. 3-in-6 chance they immediately tell everyone everything.2Help with task. Needs something fetched from dangerous/restricted area. Doesn’t understand why it’s dangerous. “Could you get this from the vault? I’m not allowed in there but YOU don’t work here so it should be fine.”3Vegetables from Aia. Will pay absurd amounts of jade coins for normal carrots.4Opinion on dispute. Two rabbits are having an argument that’s been going on for three months. They want outside perspective. The argument is deeply stupid.5Introduction to interesting person. Heard a rumor about someone (PC or NPC) and wants to meet them. Will drag you to them OR drag them to you, whichever is less convenient.6Nothing yet, but… Following you around because you’re the most interesting thing to happen in weeks. Broadcasting running commentary on everything you do to all nearby rabbits. The Royal Guards will hear within hours.

































1d6The useful information they can offer1Guard rotations: Knows when Royal Guards change shifts, which routes they take, which ones are more alert. Learned this through decades of watching.2Secret passages: “Oh, that wall? It’s not really a wall. You just push on the third crystal from the left.” Has no idea this is supposed to be secret.3Current gossip: Knows where everyone important is right now and what they’re doing. Updates in real-time via telepathy network.4Artifact locations: “That thing you’re looking for? I think it’s in the Eastern Vault. Or maybe the Archive? One of those buildings that glows purple.”5Cultural taboos: Can explain what will DEFINITELY get you caught. “Don’t touch the statues. The Princess gets really mad. Last time someone did that she petrified an entire village.”6How things work: Can explain lunar technology in terms that are technically accurate but utterly useless. “The door opens when it recognizes you. How does it recognize you? It looks at you. With its eyes. The crystal eyes. They’re in the frame.”

































1d6Rabbit problems1Lost something important. “I’ll get in trouble! I mean, it’s not that important, but it’s in a restricted area. Could you help me? I’ll help you get in if you’ll help me.”2Witnessed something unintended. Royal Guard moving artifact, classified gossip, mysterious break-in. Desperately want to tell people but I can’t, so I’m telling you.3Accidentally broke something. “Don’t tell my supervisor! I didn’t mean to! I don’t care if it’s tiny! Help me replace it, or get him to stop looking or something!“4In love with someone inappropriate. Another rabbit, a Royal Guard, possibly one of the PCs. Their telepathic yearning is embarrassing everyone.5Caught between orders. “I can’t do A because then I’m not doing B, but I can’t do B because then I’m not doing A!“6Bored beyond measure. Has done the same job for 300 years. Will absolutely help you do anything illegal if it’s interesting. Doesn’t understand consequences because nothing consequential has happened to them ever.

































1d6Rabbit jobs @1Mochi production specialist. Pounds rice all day. Very strong opinions about mochi texture.2@3Agricultural tender. Grows the deeply weird lunar crops. @4Archive assistant. Files paperwork that no one will ever read. Has access to restricted information but doesn’t realize it’s restricted.5Messenger runner. Carries physical messages between locations that are within telepathic range. The job only still exists for bureaucratic reasons.6Guard. Stands places. Not actually capable of stopping anyone. Has never encountered an actual security threat and would not know what to do.





















































2d6Today’s rumor2THE PRINCESS IS HAVING ANOTHER PARTY! She does this every month.3Hey, there’s some weird foreigners here! This is you. You have 10 minutes to an hour before the guards show up.4Someone broke into one of the vaults over at [exact vault location] yesterday. 1-in-6 chance it’s another Aian interloper.5The Guard is looking for [powerful magical item], if anyone misplaced it! Someone had a really awful reason to borrow a very dangerous tool.6Someone really screwed up. A local jobber (see jobs table) had a problem.7Did you hear about [rabbit]‘s promotion? Banal, pointless gossip.8[Rabbit name] is in love with [guard name]! Both parties mortified.9Something fell from space recently! Meteor, dead bird, celestial horror.10The Archive is doing inventory today! 3-in-6: restricted areas are especially active and watched. Else, areas are totally unwatched & chaotic.11The Princess is angry! Everyone starts panicking & hyperventilating over anything that could possibly be a problem, especially strange foreigners.12Something strange last night happened in the sky! Meteor shower, visible volcanic eruption on Aia, perfectly clear night on the Moon.

Monsters by Name
Monsters by Biome

Book-1---Glass-Empires/{-Appendix-A-}---.filePath: Book 1 - Glass Empires/{ Appendix A } - Mutations & Wounds.md
Book-1---Glass-Empires/{-Appendix-A-}---.title: { Appendix A } - Mutations & Wounds
Book-1---Glass-Empires/{-Appendix-A-}---.content:
Mutations
When a character suffers a Mutation:

The GM rolls 1d6 to choose the target nature & effects of the Mutation, shifting down the list if the rolled result is inapplicable:

1–3: The mutating effect’s description or source, if any.
4–5: The characteristics of the victim themselves.
6: The characteristics of the immediate environment surrounding the victim.


The GM rolls twice on the Mutations & Wounds Table (p. @) to determine Location, and chooses the most thematic of the two rolled results, discarding the other.
The GM determines the nature & effects of the Mutation based on the words & location & writes a description for the new Fact representing it.

Mutations are never inherently lethal & they shouldn’t prohibit continued play for PCs, but they can be very unpleasant … or quite helpful.
Wounds
When you receive a Wound, generate its location by rolling on the Mutation Location table. If you don’t have the location in question, stop; you don’t receive a Wound! Otherwise, perform an unmodifed Wound Save (straight 2d6 with no modifiers).





























ResultWound SaveCrit≥12You don’t actually suffer a Wound. You’re fine!Pass8–11The body part is merely injured & is unusable until the Wound heals.Seven7You permanently lose half your capacity (i.e. weak/unsteady leg, half your breath).Failure3–6The body part is destroyed.Fumble≤2The body part is destroyed. Roll for a second Wound location.
If the body part of the Wound isn’t destroyed, then whenever you perform the Rest Move (p. @), cross-reference the Rest Threshold for a period longer than that, the Wound is healed.
Mutations & Wounds Table















































































































































d66BodyLocationRestThresholdIf Location isDestroyed, You…11Entire head1 monthDie.12Face1 weekDie.13Arms1 monthLose 1 arm.14Hands or whatever1 monthLose 1 hand/whatever.15Legs1 monthLose 1 leg.16Feet or whatever1 monthLose 1 foot/whatever.21−22Torso1 monthDie.23−24Entire body1 seasonDie.25−26Tail1 weekLose 1 tail.31Nose1 weekCannot smell.32Ears1 weekCannot hear.33Eyes1 monthCannot see.34Most thematic organ1 month5-in-6 chance of death.35−36Horns/other protrusions1 weekLose 1 horn.41−42Skin1 weekDie painfully.43−44Mouth, tongue, or breath1 monthDie.45−46Soul/aura1 weekDie.51−52Voice1 monthCannot speak.53−54Mind/personality1 seasonDie.55Wings1 monthLose 1 wing.56Genitals1 monthCan’t reproduce.61−66Gain additional (re-roll)——

If you roll “Gain additional…” while checking for a Wound, the Wound affects two or more parts; make a Wound Save for each part’s destruction separately. If you roll it for a Mutation, the Mutation adds an additional body part (if applicable).
If you don’t have the named body part, a Mutation will add it while a Wound will do nothing & be negated.

Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-1-}---Desi.title: { 1 } - Design Philosophy @
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-1-}---Desi.content:
@ “Institutions Are People.” “Power Dynamics > Economics.” “Classes as Socioeconomic Niches.” The why behind the system. Currently scattered across the GM’s Guide as design notes. This is where the condensed versions of your theoretical frameworks live — not the full Bronze Age Optimization Trap essay, but the core insights distilled into actionable GM principles.

the importance of first-principles axioms
power always optimizes
Classes are Socioeconomic Niches
Glass Empires is About Power, Not Economics

although human power is predicated upon an economic substrate

Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-2-}---Camp.title: { 2 } - Campaigns @
Book-2---Gamemaster's-Guide/{-2-}---Camp.content:
@ How to minimally start a game. The complete worldgen pipeline: region generation, settlement placement, fortress placement, ruins & lairs, resource distribution, faction allegiance mapping, starting location selection. Currently in the GM’s Guide generators section.
Generator: Cosmos
For each table, roll 2d6; one to determine row & the second to determine column.
The sun is…








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61Devoured at dusk & reborn at dawn; visible pregnancy-bulge in the sky in the early morning.Stolen from the Moon. The thief dims & hides it every night.2A hole punched through reality; sunlight bleeds in from Elsewhere.The last heavenly flame, growing dimmer over the centuries.3The iris of a titan. Sometimes, it blinks.The golden egg of a great beast.4A burning chain of twelve linked spheres. One extinguishes each month; a new one ignites.The moon’s predator - during eclipses you can hear it feeding5A tortured prison-hell; shadows of victims move across its surface.A monstrously huge burning orb of fire.6Two visibly split birds orbiting the celestial throne.Another world, blanketed in luminous clouds.
The moon is…








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61The first city. Things still live there; mostly ghosts & rabbits.A huge chunk of the world, ripped up & into the heavens.2A mirror showing the world that should have been.A refugee from the last world, parked in the sky.3Devoured at dusk & reborn at dawn; visible pregnancy-bulge in the sky in the early morning.A coin flipped at creation to see how the world will end. It’s still spinning; that’s what phases are.4Watching us back. Each crater is an eye. They blink, if you look closely.The moon’s predator. During eclipses, you can hear it feeding.5The corpse of the sun’s twin. Beautiful, cold, plummeting slowly towards us.The skull of the first thing to die. It is said to sustain the dead.6A soon-to-bloom chrysalis. The realm’s sacrifices go towards preventing it.Close enough to fly to (if you were a bird) & much larger than it looks.
The stars are…








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61Holes in the shell that protect us from blazing chaos.Other suns, separated by great distance.2Each a god waiting to be born.Foreign anglerfish, waiting for prey.3Sparks from the forging. Each will plummet back to the ground in its own time.Each a fallen soul, wrapped in a pearl.4The campfires of the dead, lost in a vast upside-down forest.Our heroes, given peace in the heavens.5Crystallized moments of time. Telescopes can be used to spy on the past.Dreamers wandering through the night. Those who touch the moon never re-awaken.6Not to be mentioned. Do not stare at them.Gone, but for a single survivor, bless its heroic perseverence.
The sky is…








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61A thin spherical membrane separating us from an empty void.An ocean pressing down, held back by the world’s breath, & the source of all rain.2A lid, weighted with stones, to keep earthly things from heaven.The jade mechanical clockwork that keeps the sky in motion.3A canvas, painted by the gods with clouds & thunder.Normally orange-yellow, & we’re quite alarmed about this change.4A vast, thick, impenetrable mist.Thinner the further up you go, until all that remains is cold deathly vacuum.5The stomach of a goddess.Lined with great rings that slip below the horizon.6Full of clouds & lightning in all directions, without end.Tinted an alien hue, as a reminder of how we wrecked the world.
The world is…








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61A flat disc. We live on the flat top; the underworld is beneath us.An archipelago on a great ocean.2Shattered; each piece now held in place by huge bridges & iron rods.The substance at the bottom of an enormous egg.3A gigantic geometric game-board. The players abandoned the rules (& maybe the game) long ago.Shaped like an hourglass, A thin cylindrical stellar causeway connects the two worlds.4A cube, and we’re just one face.A mobius strip. You can see the other half in the sky.5A flat plane, surrounded by horrible, warped far-lands.Spherical, hanging in the sky.6A single enormous terraced mountain.A great bowl, ringed with mountains.
Supported by…








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61An ocean of liquid darkness, denser than mercury, that runs to the edge of the sky.Thirteen crystals. If they’re all broken, we’ll sink into hell, and we’ve already lost four of them.2Four titanic beasts, sentenced to carry it. Their shifts & moans make the earthquakes.A stem connected to the center. We are but a single fruit on the world-tree.3Nothing; it’s falling. The air rushing past is the wind.The orbit of our own death, circling a temporal black hole.4The unseen grip of the sun.The hand of the greatest god.5Chains anchored in the stars — visible during the day.The head of a giant who wanders the true earth.6A monolithic pillar that plummets into the pearly clouds.A tremendous spiderweb, woven by a tremendous celestial spider.
The gods are…








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61Physical, powerful, corporeal & ancient. You could become one if you tried.Incompetent creators, & we’re their bastard children.2Parasites, feeding on our worship & sacrifices.Wardens of our illusory prison, set on testing us.3Trapped in the same eternal wheel of reincarnation as we are.Just angels, scrambling to pull things together after the true god died.4On trial for creating worlds; we’re evidence, verdict pending.Like children playing; unaware of their strength & heedless of mortal life.5Refugees from the future, running from something worse.Masks & puppets, worn by an alien thing pretending to be many.6At war with each other. We’re their pawns.”Rewarding” us. This world is far better than the previous one.
When we die, we…








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61Slowly lose our spirit, and roll with the rain into the vents at the bottom of the sea.Get sorted. The celestial bureaucracy is as terrifying as it is slow.2Walk west, and leap off the edge of the world.Get audited, questioned, & sentenced by a judge.3Migrate to the Moon.Join the war in the afterlife.4Wait in the queue. It moves slowly. Nobody knows where the line ends.Slip upwards, if unweighed by commitments.5Are chained to our bodies ‘til they decay into ash.Cease to exist. Don’t worry about it.6Move to a higher — or lower — state of existence.Are sent to live on other worlds.
Also, the dead must…








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61Wander the otherworld until they find their rest.Apologize to everyone they wronged in life. You will have to wait until they all die.2Defeat their own reflection in a battle of their choosing.Confess, and have their sins carved in stone upon the walls.3Bargain with their descendants to secure good passage.Continue to labor as they did in life.4Navigate the maze. Wrong exits lead to worse hells.Avoid ravens & owls (who eat ghosts & souls) until their divine post-mail arrives.5Win the game. Death chooses the game.Somehow build a tomb, if they didn’t have one in life. A better tomb is a better afterlife.6Pay the ferryman, or suffer the consequences.Demonstrate worthiness before passing on.
Generator: Campaign World Timeline


                  
                  Tip
                  
                

For clarity, a campaign world is a chunk of the world sized approximately after a small continent — think People’s Republic of China at its maximal reaches, the Mediterranean, or the Andes & South American west coast from north to south. This might be the entirety of the world, or it might be just a small portion of it.


This generator produces the rough grasp of history that everyone in the campaign world knows. It simultaneously produces:

An explanation of the fallen precursors, who serve as an explanation for any weird powerful gadgets that the GM wants to slip into the world;
An explanation of the realm’s historical precedent for who deserves to rule & under what system of government;
A glimpse of what the world looked like when the realm was at the peak of its power;
All the myriad ways that it crumbled from within or without & lost that power;
The current condition today, including all key Factions & ongoing crises.

Glass Empires assumes that the setting is one where factional conflict is readily present, & is specifically intended for games where grand empires can be won & lost in brutal, vital hand-to-hand combat.
Millennia ago…
This step isn’t so much about the current regime (though it can be, through a long & twisted line of descendancy) so much as it is about setting the backdrop for the campaign while providing a justification for fallen, secretive relics & histories. This enables ruins & artifacts far beyond the current empires’ capabilities to be included in a game, while also allowing seemingly unexplained discoveries such as otherwise-implausible dungeons. It may also provide political justification for rule in the future — as one might always try to reclaim the glory of a distant ancestral past.








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61The apocalypse cut the Prior Age short & filled the world with ruin. The Sleepers hid us, their servants & themselves in great underground vaults … along with many lost secrets.The Singing Throne ruled through resonance, their palatial amplifiers transmitting commands as music across the continent. Your blood will still obey their hymns.2Magicians operated on Time itself, removing infected decades & transplanting better years from elsewhere. When they exhausted their fortune, an age of fire & madness ensued.The stars were known with perfect precision, recorded in humming runes throughout the mountain-peak observatories. Their secrets were copied, their treasures drawn out, & stored far underground where they’d never return to the skies.3The Lawgivers inscribed commands into the world. They thought their law would support their empire forever. Now, those laws are eternal chains that bind us in acts, promises, & patterns even today.The Sleepers learned to pull mana from dreams, & never awoke again. Ruling from the dream-world, their slaves built lavish self-ruling palaces that still loom over the landscape.4A god spoke the first language, and it became law. Those words are carved deep in their temples, & can still make stone & fire obey.Dragon teeth, soldier-seeds, & chimeric beasts let the Garden Kings take the world. Their dryad servants still rule the ruins in the wilderness.5The sky-miners hollowed out the firmament of Heaven & processed clouds like ore. Their crumbling islands & spindly towers still loom over the world.We inherited a grand civilization from our departing forefathers. We maintained but never understood it, & gradually forgot how to maintain it. The automatic castles no longer know us, & their golems think us beasts.6The First Kingdom bound servitor-spirits into every tool, building, and weapon. When it fell, the spirits remained, still following their last orders in empty halls.Great leylines were warped to build a nexus of power & an eternal kingdom. Darkness swept the hearts of the rulers, & plunged the eternal dungeon into the underworld.
Centuries ago…
This is the founding myth for the (at-present) dying world order. It is the origin of how the empire was created, from which its political legitimacy among the plebeians & priesthood is derived. It directly indicates the primary mode of government that the regime operated in the past, although over the next few hundred years, the exact details may have been twisted & altered by incentive & politics. It definitely indicates what people believe is righteous or just, in terms of governance.








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61The God-Emperor massacred the imperial court & left, leaving his children behind to clean up the mess & reconquer the Empire. The sole saint survivor founded our Heavenly Dynasty.The Seven Generals divided the continent by walking until they could no longer see each other’s banners, then planted them. Our descendants still rule where those standards fell.2The slave revolts succeeded, & the collapsing aristocracy adopted our gods in desperation. A new King was chosen, & the People became the Parliament.The Prophet-King emerged from the ruins with the blessings of the gods, & took control of the Holy Rivers of the kingdom. Now, the Hydraulic Priests kept us safe & free from drought.3The Reconstruction Army never disbanded, turning from heroism into government. Ranks became civil titles; supply lines became our tax districts.Dueling conspiracies came to war. Backed by the masses, the Moon King was overthrown & our Sun King inherited the Garden-Cities.4Bloody battle between the twins saw one finally kill the other; Victor gave meaning to the word, claimed the world, & built our Senate on his Brother’s Grave.We settled on this land with blessings of the King Back Home. The King moved to levy taxes until we’d break. We chose Glorious Revolution.5Our Father’s Tribes were led to this land, & built upon it The Temple. We knew we’d never cede it; we built Grand Walls to ensure we wouldn’t.The Admiralty built the Airships & declared all land “navigable waters” under Maritime Law. Captains became Captain-Governors; travel became trivial.6Precursor’s remains let the Academy annihilate our Rival Neighbors. With the Old King dead & the magicians the survivors, our Scholar Patriarch took to rule.Our Sea-Capital was founded on the coast, & all the waters were claimed by Merchant Fleets. Guilds became our houses, & their branches the Cadets that rule our cities to this day.
A hundred years ago…
One hundred years ago, we ruled the world. The absolute pinnacle of the golden age also feeds the empire’s rot, as every strength is a weakness once corrupted. This stage of history indicates what we used to have, which tells us the echoes of the setting’s recent history as well as the additional chaos & ruin that comes to a head with the next two generation steps.








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61Our Tributary States sent wealth beyond counting — we had warehouses for our warehouses. Domestic production withered; entire industries became ceremonial.The Imperial University educated every promising youth from every corner of the realm. Provincial talent drained to the capital; the periphery grew barren, then empty.2Our Professional Legions hadn’t lost a war in ninety years. We froze the world in peace; our Generals became famous, bloated figureheads.When the Dictator came to power, our old enemies melted before us. Conquering the Far Reaches brought us their Perpetual Harvest.3The Guilds cemented their ascendancy & built the Grand Alliance. The Silken Road grew to pass through our ports & cities, shattering our unity with a kaleidoscope of foreign wealth & belief.Our Neighboring Rival was finally defeated, & we were free to conquer the world by ship & by horse. Colonial Settlers conquered distant lands & brought their riches & lethal dangers from every corner of the world.4The Astronomical Clockwork predicted weather, harvests, & wars perfectly. Our Priests replaced old wisdom with the Calendar Codex. The government grew rigid & inhuman.Our Banks created wealth from promises of future wealth, and Guilded Lords from the mercenary-men of the past age. The Debt-Fortune funded miracles … until miracles became necessities.5Industrial fervor had turned us from a backwater into a Gilded Power. To fuel our hunger, our Alchemists drank the sea, then the skies & the earth.Subterranean surveying built the Depthmines. We breached the Veins of the Earth & found vast riches … and deep terrors.6The Printing Press grew ubiquitous, and the Magicians adored it most of all. Knowledge became common. Without scribal controls, so did heresy.The Medicine Colleges ended childhood death and plague. Our population doubled; the aging Infrastructure strained under the load.
Decades ago…
This is when the golden age truly fractured. There may have been seeds of rot beforehand, and they definitely continued (take note of the errors accumulated in the past age & exacerbate them here) but this is the shock that broke the system and sent it all tumbling down from heaven.








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61Barbarian Hordes suddenly spilled from the lands & seas, pillaging our trade & slaughtering our soldiers. Our coasts fell in days to Blood Machines we’d never imagined.The Great Earthquake shattered the capital & severed the roads. For six months, provinces governed themselves; while repairs rolled on, they acquired the taste.2The Colonial Provinces stopped sending tribute shortly after our Grand Fleet burned in a single night — sabotage said some, divine judgement said others.The Long Winter came — our winters came early, summers brief & tepid. The calendar that had worked for centuries now choked our crops, & The Great Famine followed shortly.3Our Rival Empire offered riches to any master craftsman who relocated. Within ten years, entire guild quarters stood empty, & would-be apprentices formed Thug Bands — then Warbands.The War of Five Fronts began when our uncountable enemies realized we couldn’t be everywhere at once. The Legions won every battle while losing the war.4The Grain Blight spread through the monoculture the Ministry had mandated. 60% of harvests failed; we’d already sold the Reserve Granaries to fund monuments.The Succession Crisis erupted with seventeen heirs. Parliament chose one, the Army another, the Priests a third. All had legitimate claims, and the weapons to back them.5Merchants discovered the Imperial Mint had replaced gold with lead for decades … and even the lead was borrowed. The Currency Collapse followed.The Depthmines ran dry simultaneously, as if something drank the ore-veins. The currency backed by future extraction became pretty paper. The military grew desperate. Rivals soon invaded.6The Schism split when the Hierophant declared the empire’s wealth blasphemous. They burned libraries & trade centers; converts included half our generals.The Plague proved just as lethal to our golems as to us — water-mills ground backward, clocks ran sideways, the Astronomical Clockwork started lying.
And now, just recently…
This is the ongoing crisis; the failure of the state that compels action from the PCs & creates the chaotic environment that lets force from small actors blossom into realm-scouring changes. It sets up several major players as well as opportunities for smaller factions to find fault with them & stay independent.








































1d6+ 1d61–34–61Just when we had the last crisis resolved, the Mother-Empress was declared possessed, & the Heir Son was imprisoned in the palace. Now the Four Houses & the Populist Uprising kill each other over inheritance while enemies loot the realm.The Succession War ended when all heirs were assassinated simultaneously at the peace conference. Every major faction immediately crowned their own Emperor & set themselves to war.2The chaos was brought to a head when the army split in two. The East Legions seized the ports; the West Legions the fortresses. The Palace Guards auction their loyalty to the highest bidder.A Barbarian Conquest half-succeeded, then suddenly fractured. Three Warlord-Kings claim the territory while Straggler Lords and Imperial Remnants fight both.3The Revolution slaughtered the failing government, but can’t agree on what comes next. Radical Tribunals, Foreign Intruders, & Counter Revolutionaries control fractal warzones across the land.The Provincial Governors declared independence to “restore order.” A half-dozen Petty Kings claim to be the true empire. The Free Cities recognize none of them.4The Divine Plagues killed only those of noble blood. Merchant Princes claimed abandoned titles, Slave Armies claimed their freedom, and Plague Cults claim divine mandate.The Bank Collapse brought down the realm & left 3 factions: the Merchant-Mercenaries who demand payment in land, the Debtors who refuse, and Foreign Kings financing both sides.5After decades of suffering, the Fleet mutinied and declared itself the Maritime Republic. They blockade all ports while the Land Armies starve. Smuggler Guilds grow rich between them.Raging crises finally came to a head when every god finally took sides at once. Now 4 Heavenly Temples & the Heretical Schism fight for land, cult followers, & sacrifices.6The Star-Metal fell, bringing an alien rot into the midst of our wars. Today, Crater Lords defend the sources of the infection; Prospector Crews harvest the mineral-rich toxic creep, & Star-Touched Prophets promise divinity while fighting our Capital Remnants.Driven by the madness, the Black Portal opened in the capital, disgorging eldritch Ancestral Beasts, here to take the world back. Now bloodshed rages between Loyalists & the Blackguards who betray us for promised glory.

Don’t clutter the resource rules!
